items.q: Is the marketing mix just about advertising?
items.correct: No — promotion is only one P; the mix also covers the product itself, its price and the place where it is sold, and all four must work together.
items.misconception: Marketing means the same thing as advertising.
items.random_wrong: Marketing is the department that delivers parcels.
items.why: Equating marketing with promotion is the most common GCSE marketing error.

items.q: Is internal recruitment always cheaper and better?
items.correct: It is usually cheaper and quicker and can motivate staff, but it brings no new ideas, creates another vacancy and limits the pool of candidates.
items.misconception: Promoting from within has no disadvantages.
items.random_wrong: Internal recruitment means hiring family members only.
items.why: Students need balanced evaluation of recruitment methods for the 'people' part of this component.

items.q: Does market segmentation mean ignoring some customers?
items.correct: It means targeting groups with tailored products and messages; firms may serve several segments, but focusing resources where they fit best raises effectiveness.
items.misconception: Segmentation is unfair because it excludes everyone outside the target market.
items.random_wrong: Segmentation means cutting products into smaller pieces.
items.why: Students misread targeting as exclusion rather than efficient focus of marketing spend.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SMART objectives stand for?
items.why: Correct answer: Specific. “Strategic” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which 1970 essay argued the 'social responsibility of business is to increase profits'?
items.why: Correct answer: Friedman. “Drucker” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many elements are in the SMART acronym?
items.why: The real figure is 5 elements. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
